danger_signals.visual_form: Digitized sprite graphics of Terminators and endoskeletons
danger_signals.meaning: Active threats requiring elimination; player must engage or evade

danger_signals.visual_form: Advanced liquid-metal Terminator sprite with distinct visual design
danger_signals.meaning: Boss-level threat; primary antagonist that regenerates and pursues relentlessly

danger_signals.visual_form: Human officers in uniform sprites firing weapons
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard; misguided antagonists protecting Cyberdyne facility

danger_signals.visual_form: Animated sprites of police helicopter and liquid nitrogen tanker truck
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat level; stage-specific environmental danger

danger_signals.visual_form: On-screen power gauge that decreases with sustained fire
danger_signals.meaning: Weapon effectiveness degradation; player must cease fire to regenerate

safety_signals.meaning: Objectives to protect; casualties incur point penalties but indicate mission failure state

safety_signals.visual_form: Key character sprite during escape sequences
safety_signals.meaning: Primary protected asset; his death triggers stage restart and health penalty

safety_signals.visual_form: On-screen health and secondary weapon ammunition supplies
safety_signals.meaning: Resource availability; restoration of combat capability

safety_signals.visual_form: Environmental boundaries (hideout locations, steel mill areas)
safety_signals.meaning: Safe progression points; reaching them advances narrative and avoids stage replay

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Refugee civilians and T-800 protagonist cyborg
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Hideouts, refugee gathering points, Cyberdyne Systems facility (target for destruction)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Terminators, endoskeletons, T-1000 liquid-metal prototype, hunter-killer aircraft
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Skynet headquarters in 2029, Cyberdyne Systems research facility in 1990s, steel mill (final confrontation site)

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: SWAT officers, police officers in uniform sprites
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Cyberdyne facility, police helicopter, SWAT van; present during facility infiltration stages

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Battlefield environments, industrial Skynet facility aesthetic, machinery and combat-damaged structures
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player encounters endoskeletons and Terminators; refugee protection objectives; heavy machine gun and missile loadout emphasis

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Urban environments, Cyberdyne research facility, steel mill setting, civilian and police presence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shotgun secondary weapons; SWAT officer opposition; protection of Sarah and John Connor; T-1000 liquid-nitrogen vulnerability becomes exploitable

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Equipment destruction indicators; visual feedback for destroyed lab installations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Bonus points awarded; unlocks optimal ending (Judgment Day averted); determines narrative outcome

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tanker truck spray effect; frozen Terminator sprite state
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: T-1000 temporarily immobilized; window of opportunity for final destruction; requires pushing into molten steel vat

environmental_storytelling.example: Actors from the film (Arnold Schwarzenegger as T-800, etc.) rendered as digitized graphics; establishes canonical story continuity and film authenticity
Movement from 2029 Skynet war → Cyberdyne infiltration → 1990s escape sequence; spatial geography reinforces temporal/narrative structure
Scoring system penalizes civilian deaths in early stages; refugees visually present but vulnerable; communicates moral weight of collateral damage
Cyberdyne stage allows destruction of lab equipment; visual destruction accumulation determines ending variant; spatial state affects narrative outcome
Machine gun + missiles (2029) vs. machine gun + shotgun (1990s); visual weapon swap reflects timeline shift and enemy composition changes

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Automatic firing with continuous trigger pull; power gauge visible for rate-of-fire management; regenerates weapon effectiveness when trigger released
Sprite-based movement toward player; firing weapons; on-screen presence indicates immediate threat requiring elimination
Operates police helicopter in stage 6; drives liquid nitrogen tanker in stage 7; regenerates from damage; requires specific interaction (freezing + molten steel) for permanent destruction
Return fire at player; present as environmental hazard during Cyberdyne infiltration; do not regenerate like machines but respawn in waves
Visible presence in escape sequences; death triggers stage restart and health penalty; communicates high-stakes narrative importance through mechanical consequence
Stationary in protected zones; vulnerability to fire from enemies; point penalty system ties their survival to player agency

notes: T2 arcade game uses light-gun first-person perspective with digitized actor likenesses (film tie-in advantage). Visual communication is primarily through sprite-based threat taxonomy and environmental contextual shifts between 2029 war and 1990s timeline. The casualty scoring system is unusual for arcade shooters—it penalizes player firepower indiscriminately, creating resource management tension around collateral damage. No faction color-coding documented; distinction relies on sprite asset differences and narrative context. Ending branching tied to equipment destruction metrics creates spatial state importance.
